slides.title: Labels for Your Code
slides.body: Python lets you write functions without saying what types they expect — which is flexible, but can get confusing fast. **Type hints** are optional labels that say *exactly* what kind of values a function needs and returns.
slides.tip: Python never enforces type hints at runtime — they're for you, your editor, and tools like mypy.

slides.title: Annotating Functions
slides.body: Add `: type` after each parameter name, and `-> type` before the colon that closes `def`. That's all there is to the basic syntax.
slides.tip: name: str means 'expect a string here'; -> str means 'this returns a string'.

slides.title: Typed Collections
slides.body: A bare `list` hint is okay, but `list[int]` is far more useful — it tells readers (and tools) what's *inside* the list. Same goes for `dict[str, int]`.
slides.tip: list[int] syntax works out of the box in Python 3.9+.

slides.title: Values That Might Be Missing
slides.body: Use `X | None` when a value could be absent. This is called a **union type** and signals to callers that they need to handle the `None` case.
slides.tip: str | None is the modern style (Python 3.10+); older code uses Optional[str] from typing.

slides.title: Why Bother? Real Benefits
slides.body: Type hints give you **better autocomplete**, catch bugs before you run your code (with `mypy`), and make functions self-documenting — the signature alone tells you what goes in and what comes out.
slides.tip: Install mypy with `pip install mypy` and run `mypy your_file.py` to spot type mismatches instantly.
